content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:eac54add

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: force_memory_write=true; transcript includes stable project/business facts, chosen content direction, draft copy, and reusable rationale about repurposing educational graphic design content.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements and preferences should resolve to the stable USER entity; no existing candidates.
- mention=my social media channels; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's social media channels; reason=Concrete reusable referent for the user's channels across platforms.
- mention=my website; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's website; reason=The transcript discusses the user's launched website as a durable business asset.
- mention=business plan outline; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's business plan outline; reason=A business plan outline is a durable user-created document/domain object, not source metadata.
- mention=overall business strategy; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's business strategy; reason=Reusable concept referenced as alignment target for social media.
- mention=content calendar; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's social media content calendar; reason=The user requested creation of a content calendar as a durable planning artifact.
- mention=graphic design; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Graphic design; reason=Reusable domain topic for the user's educational content.
- mention=educational content theme; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=Educational content theme; reason=Recurring content category discussed throughout the transcript.
- mention=design principles and fundamentals; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=CONCEPT; name=Design principles and fundamentals; reason=Specific reusable focus area for the user's educational content.
- mention=typography; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=CONCEPT; name=Typography; reason=Specific graphic design topic repeatedly used for blog/social posts.
- mention=Facebook post; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's typography Facebook post; reason=A concrete drafted post with specific text was discussed and revised.
- mention=Instagram post; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's typography Instagram post; reason=A concrete drafted post with specific text was discussed and revised.
- mention=blog post; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=DOCUMENT; name=User's typography blog post; reason=The transcript refers to a specific source blog post about typography being repurposed.
- mention=Facebook; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=SERVICE; name=Facebook; reason=Named social media platform used as a publishing channel.
- mention=Instagram; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=SERVICE; name=Instagram; reason=Named social media platform used as a publishing channel.
- mention=LinkedIn; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=SERVICE; name=LinkedIn; reason=Named social media platform suggested as a publishing channel.
- mention=Twitter; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=SERVICE; name=Twitter; reason=Named social media platform suggested as a publishing channel.
- mention=Canva; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<17>; type=PRODUCT; name=Canva; reason=Named tool suggested for creating visuals.
- mention=Adobe Creative Cloud; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<18>; type=PRODUCT; name=Adobe Creative Cloud; reason=Named creative tool suite suggested for visuals and tutorials.
- mention=Hootsuite; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<19>; type=PRODUCT; name=Hootsuite; reason=Named tool suggested for social media content creation/scheduling.
- mention=Buffer; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<20>; type=PRODUCT; name=Buffer; reason=Named scheduling tool suggested for social media posts.
- mention=Sprout Social; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<21>; type=PRODUCT; name=Sprout Social; reason=Named scheduling/social media management tool suggested.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<22>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:eac54add:session:1; title=LongMemEval eac54add session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/02/10 (Fri) 10:37
Transcript:
user: I need help with creating a content calendar for my social media channels. I want to make sure I'm consistently posting engaging content that will attract potential clients. By the way, I just launched my website and created a business plan outline, so I want to make sure my social media aligns with my overall business strategy.
assistant: Congratulations on launching your website and creating a business plan outline! Developing a content calendar for your social media channels is a great next step to ensure consistency and alignment with your overall business strategy. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you create a content calendar that attracts potential clients:

**1. Identify your target audience:**
Review your business plan and identify your ideal client persona(s). Who are they? What are their pain points, interests, and goals? Understanding your audience will help you create content that resonates with them.

**2. Determine your content themes:**
Based on your business plan and target audience, identify 3-5 content themes that align with your business goals and appeal to your audience. For example:
	* Educational content (e.g., industry tips, best practices)
	* Behind-the-scenes content (e.g., company news, team spotlights)
	* Promotional content (e.g., product/services showcases, special offers)
	* Inspirational/motivational content (e.g., success stories, quotes)
	* Engaging/entertaining content (e.g., quizzes, polls, fun facts)

**3. Choose your social media channels:**
Select the social media platforms where your target audience is most active. You may want to focus on 2-3 channels to start, such as Facebook, Instagram, and LinkedIn.

**4. Set your posting frequency:**
Decide how often you want to post on each channel. A general rule of thumb is:
	* Facebook: 3-5 posts per week
	* Instagram: 5-7 posts per week
	* LinkedIn: 2-3 posts per week

**5. Plan your content in advance:**
Use a calendar or planner to schedule your content for the next 4-6 weeks. You can use a template or create your own. Consider the following:
	* Identify specific dates for posting (e.g., every Monday, Wednesday, Friday)
	* Allocate specific themes to each day (e.g., Monday: Educational, Wednesday: Behind-the-scenes)
	* Plan for special events, holidays, or awareness months (e.g., National Small Business Month)

**6. Create a content library:**
Develop a library of content ideas, including:
	* Blog posts or articles
	* Images (e.g., photos, infographics, quotes)
	* Videos (e.g., tutorials, testimonials, company updates)
	* Curated content from other sources (e.g., industry news, relevant articles)

**7. Write engaging captions and descriptions:**
Craft attention-grabbing headlines, captions, and descriptions for each post. Make sure they're concise, informative, and aligned with your brand voice.

**8. Visualize your content:**
Use a tool like Canva, Adobe Creative Cloud, or Hootsuite to create visually appealing graphics, images, and videos that align with your brand identity.

**9. Schedule and automate:**
Use a scheduling tool like Hootsuite, Buffer, or Sprout Social to schedule your posts in advance. This will save you time and ensure consistency.

**10. Monitor and adjust:**
Track your analytics to see which content performs well and adjust your content calendar accordingly. Engage with your audience, respond to comments, and make changes to your content strategy as needed.

Here's an example of what your content calendar might look like:

| Date | Channel | Theme | Content |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Mon, March 7 | Facebook | Educational | "5 Tips for Improving Your Website's SEO" (blog post) |
| Wed, March 9 | Instagram | Behind-the-scenes | "Meet our team: [Team Member]" (photo and bio) |
| Fri, March 11 | LinkedIn | Promotional | "Introducing our new service: [Service Name]" (video) |

Remember to stay flexible and adapt your content
user: I'd like to focus on the educational content theme. Can you suggest some topics related to graphic design that I can create content around? I want to establish myself as an expert in my field.
assistant: Educational content is a fantastic way to showcase your expertise and attract potential clients. Here are some topic ideas related to graphic design that can help you establish yourself as an expert in your field:

**Design Principles and Fundamentals**

1. The importance of typography in design: Share tips on font selection, pairing, and best practices.
2. Color theory 101: Explain the color wheel, color harmony, and how to create a color palette.
3. Balancing composition: Discuss the rule of thirds, symmetry, and negative space.
4. Understanding design hierarchy: How to guide the viewer's attention through visual elements.

**Design Trends and Inspiration**

1. Top design trends for [current year]: Highlight emerging trends, and provide examples of how to incorporate them into designs.
2. Design inspiration from [specific industry or style]: Share examples of inspiring designs from various industries or styles, and how to apply those principles to other projects.
3. How to create a mood board: Walk readers through the process of creating a mood board, and how it helps with design direction.

**Design Process and Workflow**

1. My design process: Share your personal design process, from concept to delivery, and the tools you use.
2. How to create a design brief: Provide a template and guide on creating a design brief, and why it's essential for successful projects.
3. Tips for effective design feedback: Offer advice on how to give and receive constructive feedback during the design process.

**Design Tools and Software**

1. Top 5 design tools for [specific task or industry]: Share your favorite tools and plugins for tasks like logo design, icon creation, or UI design.
2. Adobe Creative Cloud tutorial: Create a step-by-step guide on how to use a specific Adobe tool, such as Photoshop or Illustrator.
3. How to choose the right design software for your business: Compare popular design tools and provide guidance on selecting the best one for specific needs.

**Design for Specific Industries or Applications**

1. Designing for accessibility: Share best practices for creating accessible designs, including color contrast, typography, and imagery.
2. The importance of branding for small businesses: Explain how a strong brand identity can benefit small businesses, and provide tips on creating a consistent brand.
3. Designing for e-commerce: Offer advice on designing effective e-commerce websites, including product pages, checkout processes, and mobile optimization.

**Common Design Mistakes and Solutions**

1. Top 5 design mistakes and how to avoid them: Identify common design mistakes and provide solutions or alternatives.
2. How to fix a design that's not working: Share your process for identifying and resolving design issues.
3. Designing for different screen sizes and devices: Offer tips on creating responsive designs that work across various devices and screen sizes.

**Case Studies and Success Stories**

1. Behind-the-scenes of a successful design project: Share a case study of a project you worked on, including the design process, challenges, and results.
2. How design improved [specific business metric]: Highlight a project where design had a significant impact on a business metric, such as conversion rates or engagement.
3. Designing for a specific industry or niche: Share your experience designing for a particular industry, and the unique challenges and opportunities that come with it.

These topics should give you a great starting point for creating educational content that showcases your expertise in graphic design. Remember to keep your content informative, engaging, and concise, and to use visuals to illustrate your points. Good luck!
user: I like these topic ideas. Can you suggest some ways to repurpose this content across different social media channels? For example, how can I turn a blog post into a Facebook post or an Instagram story?
assistant: Repurposing content is a great way to maximize your content's reach and minimize the time spent creating new content. Here are some ideas to repurpose your educational graphic design content across different social media channels:

**Blog Post → Facebook Post**

1. Summarize the blog post: Share a brief summary of the blog post, highlighting the main points and takeaways.
2. Share a key takeaway: Extract a valuable tip or quote from the blog post and share it as a Facebook post.
3. Ask a question: Pose a question related to the blog post and ask your audience to share their thoughts or experiences.
4. Share a visual: Share an infographic, image, or graphic from the blog post and provide a brief description.

**Blog Post → Instagram Post**

1. Visual summary: Create a visually appealing image summarizing the blog post, using icons, graphics, or quotes.
2. Quote graphic: Design a quote graphic featuring a key takeaway or inspiring quote from the blog post.
3. Sneak peek: Share a sneak peek of the blog post, highlighting a specific section or image.
4. Instagram Carousel: Break down the blog post into a series of 3-5 images, each highlighting a key point or takeaway.

**Blog Post → Instagram Story**

1. Swipe-up link: Share a brief summary of the blog post and add a swipe-up link to drive traffic to the blog post.
2. Sneak peek: Share a sneak peek of the blog post, highlighting a specific section or image.
3. Q&A: Share a question related to the blog post and ask your audience to answer in the comments or via DM.
4. Behind-the-scenes: Share a behind-the-scenes look at how you created the blog post, including research, writing, or design process.

**Blog Post → LinkedIn Post**

1. Professional summary: Share a summary of the blog post, highlighting the key takeaways and how they apply to professionals in the industry.
2. Industry insights: Share insights from the blog post that are relevant to your industry or niche.
3. Thought leadership: Share your expertise and thought leadership on the topic, highlighting your unique perspective.
4. Call to action: Encourage your LinkedIn audience to share their thoughts or experiences related to the blog post.

**Blog Post → Twitter Thread**

1. Break it down: Break down the blog post into a series of 3-5 tweets, each highlighting a key point or takeaway.
2. Key takeaways: Share the main takeaways from the blog post, using Twitter's character limit to your advantage.
3. Quote tweet: Share a quote from the blog post and add your own thoughts or commentary.
4. Ask a question: Pose a question related to the blog post and ask your Twitter audience to share their thoughts or experiences.

**Blog Post → Video Content**

1. Animated explainer: Create an animated explainer video summarizing the blog post.
2. Screen recording: Record a screen recording of yourself walking through the blog post, highlighting key points and takeaways.
3. Live streaming: Host a live streaming session discussing the blog post, answering questions, and providing additional insights.
4. Interview-style video: Create an interview-style video discussing the blog post with an expert or thought leader in the industry.

Remember to tailor your content to each social media platform's unique audience and format. By repurposing your content, you can maximize your reach and engagement while minimizing the time spent creating new content.
user: I like these ideas. I think I'll start by turning one of my blog posts into a Facebook post and an Instagram post. I'll choose a blog post that's related to design principles and fundamentals, since that's what I want to focus on for my educational content theme. Do you think I should share a summary of the blog post on Facebook, or share a key takeaway with a visual? And for Instagram, should I create a visual summary or a quote graphic?
assistant: For Facebook, I would recommend sharing a key takeaway with a visual. This will help grab the attention of your audience and make the content more engaging. A summary of the blog post might be too lengthy for Facebook, and might not encourage as much engagement.

For example, let's say your blog post is about the importance of typography in design. You could share a key takeaway like:

"Did you know that typography can make or break the readability of your design? Choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience. Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design! [link to blog post]"

You could pair this with a visual that showcases different fonts, or an image that demonstrates the impact of typography on design.

For Instagram, I would recommend creating a visual summary. Instagram is a visually-driven platform, and a visual summary will help your audience quickly understand the main points of your blog post. You could create an infographic, a series of images, or even a carousel post that breaks down the key takeaways from your blog post.

For example, you could create a series of 3 images that illustrate the importance of typography in design. The first image could show a design with poor typography, the second image could show the same design with improved typography, and the third image could provide a quote or statistic about the impact of typography on design.

A quote graphic could also work well on Instagram, but it might not be as engaging as a visual summary. However, if you have a powerful quote that sums up the main point of your blog post, it could be a great way to drive engagement and encourage people to read the full post.

Remember to keep your visuals consistent with your brand, and make sure they're high-quality and easy to read. Good luck!
user: I think I'll go with sharing a key takeaway with a visual on Facebook, and creating a visual summary on Instagram. I like the idea of using an image that demonstrates the impact of typography on design for Facebook, and creating a series of images that illustrate the importance of typography in design for Instagram.

Here's a possible Facebook post:

"Typography matters! Did you know that choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience? Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design and how you can improve your design skills! [link to blog post] #typography #designprinciples #graphicdesign"

And here's a possible Instagram post:

"Typography 101 Want to take your design skills to the next level? It starts with typography! Swipe through to learn how typography can make or break your design, and how to choose the right font for your project. Read the full blog post in the link in our bio! #typography #designfund
assistant: I love it! Your Facebook post is concise, informative, and visually appealing. The use of "Typography matters!" as a hook will grab the attention of your audience, and the image will help to illustrate the impact of typography on design. The call-to-action to check out the blog post is clear and direct.

Your Instagram post is also great! The use of "Typography 101" as a title is catchy and educational, and the series of images will help to break down the importance of typography in design in a visually appealing way. The swipe-through format will encourage engagement and make the content more interactive. The call-to-action to read the full blog post is clear, and the use of relevant hashtags will help to increase the post's discoverability.

Some minor suggestions I have are:

* On Facebook, you could add a few more hashtags to reach a wider audience. For example, you could add #graphicdesign, #designinspiration, or #uxdesign.
* On Instagram, you could consider adding a few more visuals to the series to make it more comprehensive. For example, you could add an image that shows a design with poor typography, an image that shows the same design with improved typography, and an image that provides a quote or statistic about the impact of typography on design.

Overall, however, your posts are well-crafted and will likely resonate with your audience. Good job!
user: I think I'll add a few more hashtags to the Facebook post to reach a wider audience. And for Instagram, I'll consider adding a few more visuals to make the series more comprehensive.

Here's the revised Facebook post:

"Typography matters! Did you know that choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience? Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design and how you can improve your design skills! [link to blog post] #typography #designprinciples #graphicdesign #uxdesign #designinspiration"

And here's the revised Instagram post:

"Typography 101 Want to take your design skills to the next level? It starts with typography! Swipe through to learn how typography can make or break your design, and how to choose the right font for your project. From readability to emotional response, typography plays a crucial role in design. Read the full blog post in the link in our bio! #typography #designfundamentals
assistant: I think you're good to go! Your revised Facebook post is concise, informative, and visually appealing, with a clear call-to-action to check out the blog post. The additional hashtags will help you reach a wider audience interested in design principles, graphic design, UX design, and design inspiration.

Your revised Instagram post is also great! The series of images will help to break down the importance of typography in design in a visually appealing way, and the additional visuals will make the series more comprehensive. The use of "From readability to emotional response, typography plays a crucial role in design" as a caption will help to provide context and encourage users to swipe through the series.

Remember to track your analytics to see how your posts perform, and adjust your strategy accordingly. You may want to experiment with different hashtags, visuals, or captions to see what resonates best with your audience.

Also, don't forget to engage with your audience by responding to comments and direct messages. This will help to build a community around your brand and increase engagement with your content.

Good luck with your social media campaign!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.

        Router reason: force_memory_write=true; transcript includes stable project/business facts, chosen content direction, draft copy, and reusable rationale about repurposing educational graphic design content.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/02/10 (Fri) 10:37
Transcript:
user: I need help with creating a content calendar for my social media channels. I want to make sure I'm consistently posting engaging content that will attract potential clients. By the way, I just launched my website and created a business plan outline, so I want to make sure my social media aligns with my overall business strategy.
assistant: Congratulations on launching your website and creating a business plan outline! Developing a content calendar for your social media channels is a great next step to ensure consistency and alignment with your overall business strategy. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you create a content calendar that attracts potential clients:

**1. Identify your target audience:**
Review your business plan and identify your ideal client persona(s). Who are they? What are their pain points, interests, and goals? Understanding your audience will help you create content that resonates with them.

**2. Determine your content themes:**
Based on your business plan and target audience, identify 3-5 content themes that align with your business goals and appeal to your audience. For example:
	* Educational content (e.g., industry tips, best practices)
	* Behind-the-scenes content (e.g., company news, team spotlights)
	* Promotional content (e.g., product/services showcases, special offers)
	* Inspirational/motivational content (e.g., success stories, quotes)
	* Engaging/entertaining content (e.g., quizzes, polls, fun facts)

**3. Choose your social media channels:**
Select the social media platforms where your target audience is most active. You may want to focus on 2-3 channels to start, such as Facebook, Instagram, and LinkedIn.

**4. Set your posting frequency:**
Decide how often you want to post on each channel. A general rule of thumb is:
	* Facebook: 3-5 posts per week
	* Instagram: 5-7 posts per week
	* LinkedIn: 2-3 posts per week

**5. Plan your content in advance:**
Use a calendar or planner to schedule your content for the next 4-6 weeks. You can use a template or create your own. Consider the following:
	* Identify specific dates for posting (e.g., every Monday, Wednesday, Friday)
	* Allocate specific themes to each day (e.g., Monday: Educational, Wednesday: Behind-the-scenes)
	* Plan for special events, holidays, or awareness months (e.g., National Small Business Month)

**6. Create a content library:**
Develop a library of content ideas, including:
	* Blog posts or articles
	* Images (e.g., photos, infographics, quotes)
	* Videos (e.g., tutorials, testimonials, company updates)
	* Curated content from other sources (e.g., industry news, relevant articles)

**7. Write engaging captions and descriptions:**
Craft attention-grabbing headlines, captions, and descriptions for each post. Make sure they're concise, informative, and aligned with your brand voice.

**8. Visualize your content:**
Use a tool like Canva, Adobe Creative Cloud, or Hootsuite to create visually appealing graphics, images, and videos that align with your brand identity.

**9. Schedule and automate:**
Use a scheduling tool like Hootsuite, Buffer, or Sprout Social to schedule your posts in advance. This will save you time and ensure consistency.

**10. Monitor and adjust:**
Track your analytics to see which content performs well and adjust your content calendar accordingly. Engage with your audience, respond to comments, and make changes to your content strategy as needed.

Here's an example of what your content calendar might look like:

| Date | Channel | Theme | Content |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Mon, March 7 | Facebook | Educational | "5 Tips for Improving Your Website's SEO" (blog post) |
| Wed, March 9 | Instagram | Behind-the-scenes | "Meet our team: [Team Member]" (photo and bio) |
| Fri, March 11 | LinkedIn | Promotional | "Introducing our new service: [Service Name]" (video) |

Remember to stay flexible and adapt your content
user: I'd like to focus on the educational content theme. Can you suggest some topics related to graphic design that I can create content around? I want to establish myself as an expert in my field.
assistant: Educational content is a fantastic way to showcase your expertise and attract potential clients. Here are some topic ideas related to graphic design that can help you establish yourself as an expert in your field:

**Design Principles and Fundamentals**

1. The importance of typography in design: Share tips on font selection, pairing, and best practices.
2. Color theory 101: Explain the color wheel, color harmony, and how to create a color palette.
3. Balancing composition: Discuss the rule of thirds, symmetry, and negative space.
4. Understanding design hierarchy: How to guide the viewer's attention through visual elements.

**Design Trends and Inspiration**

1. Top design trends for [current year]: Highlight emerging trends, and provide examples of how to incorporate them into designs.
2. Design inspiration from [specific industry or style]: Share examples of inspiring designs from various industries or styles, and how to apply those principles to other projects.
3. How to create a mood board: Walk readers through the process of creating a mood board, and how it helps with design direction.

**Design Process and Workflow**

1. My design process: Share your personal design process, from concept to delivery, and the tools you use.
2. How to create a design brief: Provide a template and guide on creating a design brief, and why it's essential for successful projects.
3. Tips for effective design feedback: Offer advice on how to give and receive constructive feedback during the design process.

**Design Tools and Software**

1. Top 5 design tools for [specific task or industry]: Share your favorite tools and plugins for tasks like logo design, icon creation, or UI design.
2. Adobe Creative Cloud tutorial: Create a step-by-step guide on how to use a specific Adobe tool, such as Photoshop or Illustrator.
3. How to choose the right design software for your business: Compare popular design tools and provide guidance on selecting the best one for specific needs.

**Design for Specific Industries or Applications**

1. Designing for accessibility: Share best practices for creating accessible designs, including color contrast, typography, and imagery.
2. The importance of branding for small businesses: Explain how a strong brand identity can benefit small businesses, and provide tips on creating a consistent brand.
3. Designing for e-commerce: Offer advice on designing effective e-commerce websites, including product pages, checkout processes, and mobile optimization.

**Common Design Mistakes and Solutions**

1. Top 5 design mistakes and how to avoid them: Identify common design mistakes and provide solutions or alternatives.
2. How to fix a design that's not working: Share your process for identifying and resolving design issues.
3. Designing for different screen sizes and devices: Offer tips on creating responsive designs that work across various devices and screen sizes.

**Case Studies and Success Stories**

1. Behind-the-scenes of a successful design project: Share a case study of a project you worked on, including the design process, challenges, and results.
2. How design improved [specific business metric]: Highlight a project where design had a significant impact on a business metric, such as conversion rates or engagement.
3. Designing for a specific industry or niche: Share your experience designing for a particular industry, and the unique challenges and opportunities that come with it.

These topics should give you a great starting point for creating educational content that showcases your expertise in graphic design. Remember to keep your content informative, engaging, and concise, and to use visuals to illustrate your points. Good luck!
user: I like these topic ideas. Can you suggest some ways to repurpose this content across different social media channels? For example, how can I turn a blog post into a Facebook post or an Instagram story?
assistant: Repurposing content is a great way to maximize your content's reach and minimize the time spent creating new content. Here are some ideas to repurpose your educational graphic design content across different social media channels:

**Blog Post → Facebook Post**

1. Summarize the blog post: Share a brief summary of the blog post, highlighting the main points and takeaways.
2. Share a key takeaway: Extract a valuable tip or quote from the blog post and share it as a Facebook post.
3. Ask a question: Pose a question related to the blog post and ask your audience to share their thoughts or experiences.
4. Share a visual: Share an infographic, image, or graphic from the blog post and provide a brief description.

**Blog Post → Instagram Post**

1. Visual summary: Create a visually appealing image summarizing the blog post, using icons, graphics, or quotes.
2. Quote graphic: Design a quote graphic featuring a key takeaway or inspiring quote from the blog post.
3. Sneak peek: Share a sneak peek of the blog post, highlighting a specific section or image.
4. Instagram Carousel: Break down the blog post into a series of 3-5 images, each highlighting a key point or takeaway.

**Blog Post → Instagram Story**

1. Swipe-up link: Share a brief summary of the blog post and add a swipe-up link to drive traffic to the blog post.
2. Sneak peek: Share a sneak peek of the blog post, highlighting a specific section or image.
3. Q&A: Share a question related to the blog post and ask your audience to answer in the comments or via DM.
4. Behind-the-scenes: Share a behind-the-scenes look at how you created the blog post, including research, writing, or design process.

**Blog Post → LinkedIn Post**

1. Professional summary: Share a summary of the blog post, highlighting the key takeaways and how they apply to professionals in the industry.
2. Industry insights: Share insights from the blog post that are relevant to your industry or niche.
3. Thought leadership: Share your expertise and thought leadership on the topic, highlighting your unique perspective.
4. Call to action: Encourage your LinkedIn audience to share their thoughts or experiences related to the blog post.

**Blog Post → Twitter Thread**

1. Break it down: Break down the blog post into a series of 3-5 tweets, each highlighting a key point or takeaway.
2. Key takeaways: Share the main takeaways from the blog post, using Twitter's character limit to your advantage.
3. Quote tweet: Share a quote from the blog post and add your own thoughts or commentary.
4. Ask a question: Pose a question related to the blog post and ask your Twitter audience to share their thoughts or experiences.

**Blog Post → Video Content**

1. Animated explainer: Create an animated explainer video summarizing the blog post.
2. Screen recording: Record a screen recording of yourself walking through the blog post, highlighting key points and takeaways.
3. Live streaming: Host a live streaming session discussing the blog post, answering questions, and providing additional insights.
4. Interview-style video: Create an interview-style video discussing the blog post with an expert or thought leader in the industry.

Remember to tailor your content to each social media platform's unique audience and format. By repurposing your content, you can maximize your reach and engagement while minimizing the time spent creating new content.
user: I like these ideas. I think I'll start by turning one of my blog posts into a Facebook post and an Instagram post. I'll choose a blog post that's related to design principles and fundamentals, since that's what I want to focus on for my educational content theme. Do you think I should share a summary of the blog post on Facebook, or share a key takeaway with a visual? And for Instagram, should I create a visual summary or a quote graphic?
assistant: For Facebook, I would recommend sharing a key takeaway with a visual. This will help grab the attention of your audience and make the content more engaging. A summary of the blog post might be too lengthy for Facebook, and might not encourage as much engagement.

For example, let's say your blog post is about the importance of typography in design. You could share a key takeaway like:

"Did you know that typography can make or break the readability of your design? Choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience. Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design! [link to blog post]"

You could pair this with a visual that showcases different fonts, or an image that demonstrates the impact of typography on design.

For Instagram, I would recommend creating a visual summary. Instagram is a visually-driven platform, and a visual summary will help your audience quickly understand the main points of your blog post. You could create an infographic, a series of images, or even a carousel post that breaks down the key takeaways from your blog post.

For example, you could create a series of 3 images that illustrate the importance of typography in design. The first image could show a design with poor typography, the second image could show the same design with improved typography, and the third image could provide a quote or statistic about the impact of typography on design.

A quote graphic could also work well on Instagram, but it might not be as engaging as a visual summary. However, if you have a powerful quote that sums up the main point of your blog post, it could be a great way to drive engagement and encourage people to read the full post.

Remember to keep your visuals consistent with your brand, and make sure they're high-quality and easy to read. Good luck!
user: I think I'll go with sharing a key takeaway with a visual on Facebook, and creating a visual summary on Instagram. I like the idea of using an image that demonstrates the impact of typography on design for Facebook, and creating a series of images that illustrate the importance of typography in design for Instagram.

Here's a possible Facebook post:

"Typography matters! Did you know that choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience? Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design and how you can improve your design skills! [link to blog post] #typography #designprinciples #graphicdesign"

And here's a possible Instagram post:

"Typography 101 Want to take your design skills to the next level? It starts with typography! Swipe through to learn how typography can make or break your design, and how to choose the right font for your project. Read the full blog post in the link in our bio! #typography #designfund
assistant: I love it! Your Facebook post is concise, informative, and visually appealing. The use of "Typography matters!" as a hook will grab the attention of your audience, and the image will help to illustrate the impact of typography on design. The call-to-action to check out the blog post is clear and direct.

Your Instagram post is also great! The use of "Typography 101" as a title is catchy and educational, and the series of images will help to break down the importance of typography in design in a visually appealing way. The swipe-through format will encourage engagement and make the content more interactive. The call-to-action to read the full blog post is clear, and the use of relevant hashtags will help to increase the post's discoverability.

Some minor suggestions I have are:

* On Facebook, you could add a few more hashtags to reach a wider audience. For example, you could add #graphicdesign, #designinspiration, or #uxdesign.
* On Instagram, you could consider adding a few more visuals to the series to make it more comprehensive. For example, you could add an image that shows a design with poor typography, an image that shows the same design with improved typography, and an image that provides a quote or statistic about the impact of typography on design.

Overall, however, your posts are well-crafted and will likely resonate with your audience. Good job!
user: I think I'll add a few more hashtags to the Facebook post to reach a wider audience. And for Instagram, I'll consider adding a few more visuals to make the series more comprehensive.

Here's the revised Facebook post:

"Typography matters! Did you know that choosing the right font can increase engagement and improve user experience? Check out our latest blog post to learn more about the power of typography in design and how you can improve your design skills! [link to blog post] #typography #designprinciples #graphicdesign #uxdesign #designinspiration"

And here's the revised Instagram post:

"Typography 101 Want to take your design skills to the next level? It starts with typography! Swipe through to learn how typography can make or break your design, and how to choose the right font for your project. From readability to emotional response, typography plays a crucial role in design. Read the full blog post in the link in our bio! #typography #designfundamentals
assistant: I think you're good to go! Your revised Facebook post is concise, informative, and visually appealing, with a clear call-to-action to check out the blog post. The additional hashtags will help you reach a wider audience interested in design principles, graphic design, UX design, and design inspiration.

Your revised Instagram post is also great! The series of images will help to break down the importance of typography in design in a visually appealing way, and the additional visuals will make the series more comprehensive. The use of "From readability to emotional response, typography plays a crucial role in design" as a caption will help to provide context and encourage users to swipe through the series.

Remember to track your analytics to see how your posts perform, and adjust your strategy accordingly. You may want to experiment with different hashtags, visuals, or captions to see what resonates best with your audience.

Also, don't forget to engage with your audience by responding to comments and direct messages. This will help to build a community around your brand and increase engagement with your content.

Good luck with your social media campaign!
